Paper_Metadata.Paper_Title: Highly printable, strong, and ductile ordered intermetallic alloy

Composition_Info.Note: Pre-alloyed powder nominal composition. As-printed composition not measured separately in text.

Process_Text.original: The CCIMA samples were manufactured using a commercial SLM®125HL LPBF machine equipped with an IPG fiber laser with a maximum power of 400 W and a laser beam diameter of 64 μm. The LPBF process was conducted in an argon-protective atmosphere with an oxygen content below 400 ppm. The 316L stainless steel used as the printing substrate was heated up to 200°C to reduce the residual stress during printing. To investigate the effects of scanning speed on the forming ability of the as-printed CCIMA, the scanning speeds varied from 500 to 1000 mm/s with a permanent increasement of 100 mm/s. The layer thickness of 0.03 mm, hatch distance of 0.12 mm and a laser-beam rotation of 67° for the consecutive layers were fixed for all the samples. The tensile samples were directly printed using the optimized laser power of 200 W and scanning speed of 900 mm/s. Flat dog-bone-shaped tensile samples with a gauge length of 12.5 mm and a cross-section dimension of ~3.2×3 mm² were directly fabricated by the LPBF. The as-printed samples were cut off from the 316L stainless steel substrate using the wire electrical discharge machining.
Process_Text.simplified: LPBF using SLM®125HL machine with 200 W laser power, 900 mm/s scan speed, 30 μm layer thickness, 120 μm hatch spacing, 64 μm spot diameter, argon atmosphere (<400 ppm O2), build plate preheated to 200°C, 67° rotation between layers.

Process_Info.Equipment: SLM®125HL LPBF machine (IPG fiber laser, max 400 W)

Microstructure_Text.original: Nearly fully dense samples were well obtained when the laser scanning speeds varied from 600 to 1000 mm/s. The micro-CT result of the sample fabricated at a laser scanning speed of 900 mm/s shows an average pore diameter of 22.0 μm. Only a few pore defects with a low porosity of 0.005% were detected in the as-printed CCIMA. In particular, there are no micro-cracks. The as-printed microstructure contains a dendritic microstructure consisting of brittle G phase.
Microstructure_Text.simplified: As-printed CCIMA shows low porosity (0.005%), average pore diameter 22.0 μm, no micro-cracks. Contains dendritic brittle G phase.

Composition_Info.Note: Measured composition after thermal stabilization; B not detected by SEM-EDS.

Process_Info.Process_Category: Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF) + Thermal Stabilization

Process_Text.original: The as-printed CCIMAs are subjected to a single-step thermal stabilization treatment (1000°C / 2h). Thermal stabilization treatment was conducted at 1000°C for 2h followed by air cooling to eliminate the non-equilibrium brittle phase and residual stress.
Process_Text.simplified: Thermal stabilization at 1000°C for 2 h followed by air cooling.

Microstructure_Text.original: XRD spectrum with obvious superlattice peaks confirms the formation of a highly ordered L1₂-type crystal structure without detecting any other detrimental phases. EBSD-IPF map shows random grain distribution with a maximum texture index of 2.56. Average grain size is 17.7 μm. BSE image together with EDS maps demonstrate fully equiaxed grains with homogeneous elemental distributions. TEM-BF image illustrates that the dendritic microstructure consisting of brittle G phase existed in the as-printed CCIMA was effectively eliminated after the simple thermal treatment. Obvious superlattice spots in the SAED pattern indicate a highly ordered structure of the grains. 3D-APT reveals homogeneous compositional distributions without undesired elemental clustering or precipitation in an atomic scale. AC-STEM HAADF image and atomic-resolution EDS probe the spatial site-occupancy behavior: Ni and Co occupy faced centers (A-type sublattice), Si, Ti, Al occupy vertices (B-type sublattice) of L1₂ unit cell, forming (Ni,Co)₃(Si,Ti,Al)-type chemically complex ordered superlattice structure. A distinct nanolayer near the grain boundary is rich in Co and B, depleted in Ni, Si, Ti, Al. The nanolayer is a disordered FCC solid-solution structure (γ phase) coherent with adjacent ordered L1₂ grain. Thickness of DINL is about 5 nm. Volume fraction of DINL estimated as 0.08%.
Microstructure_Text.simplified: Thermal-stabilized CCIMA exhibits fully ordered L1₂ structure (γ' phase) with average grain size 17.7 μm. Grain boundaries decorated with ~5 nm thick disordered FCC interfacial nanolayer (DINL) rich in Co and B (volume fraction ~0.08%). No brittle G phase remains.

Composition_Info.Note: Reference data from Fig. 1e, citing studies on Ni3Al-based alloys fabricated by LPBF. Exact composition not detailed in this paper.

Process_Text.original: Referenced from studies on Ni3Al-based intermetallic alloys fabricated by LPBF (citations 9,10).

Microstructure_Text.original: Conventional intermetallic alloys with simple chemistries, such as Ni3Al, suffer from severe cracking during LPBF.
Microstructure_Text.simplified: Ni3Al-based intermetallic exhibits cracking issues during LPBF.

Process_Text.original: Referenced from studies on TiAl intermetallic alloys fabricated by additive manufacturing (laser melting deposition, selective electron beam melting).

Microstructure_Text.original: TiAl alloys are conventional intermetallics with limited printability and ductility.
